hook: You already know that multiplying numbers is powerful — but what happens when you multiply the same number by itself over and over again? A single bacterium doubling every hour, a savings account growing with compound interest, a radioactive material slowly fading away: these patterns can't be captured by a straight line. In this unit, you'll unlock the mathematics behind explosive growth and steady decay.
concept: Every topic in this unit connects to one big idea: what happens when a quantity changes by a constant *ratio* instead of a constant difference. This unifying structure is called an *exponential relationship*, and it appears in nature, finance, science, and technology.

topic_roadmap.description: Master the rules — product, quotient, and power properties — that let you simplify and manipulate exponential expressions efficiently.
You will learn why $x^0 = 1$ and what a negative exponent like $x^{-3}$ really means, extending the exponent rules to all integers.
Apply your exponent skills to write extremely large or small numbers in the compact form $a \times 10^n$, and perform calculations with them.
Model real-world situations — like population booms or compound interest — using functions of the form $f(x) = a(1+r)^x$ where quantities increase by a constant percentage.
Analyze situations where quantities shrink by a constant factor over time, such as drug concentration or depreciation, using functions of the form $f(x) = a(1-r)^x$.
Identify and extend sequences where each term is found by multiplying the previous term by a fixed ratio, and write explicit and recursive formulas for them.
You will develop the judgment to decide whether a data set is best modeled by a linear or exponential function, and explain what each model predicts over time.
